subject: ap-world-history
theme: Between 1900 and 1945, accelerating imperial competition, extreme nationalist ideologies, and the unresolved tensions of World War I produced a second global conflict of unprecedented destruction — marked by total war mobilization, mass atrocities, and economic crises that discredited democratic and liberal institutions across Europe and beyond.
core_concepts: World War I resulted from the intersection of long-term structural causes (imperial competition, the alliance system, militarism, and nationalism) and short-term triggers (the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand) — creating a conflict that quickly escalated into a total war involving civilian mobilization, industrial-scale killing, and trench warfare stalemate on the Western Front.

Total war required states to mobilize entire economies and societies for military purposes: governments controlled production, rationed food, censored news, and deployed propaganda to maintain civilian support — transforming the relationship between states and citizens in all belligerent nations and accelerating the expansion of state authority over economic life.

The Treaty of Versailles (1919) imposed punishing reparations, territorial losses, and the 'war guilt' clause on Germany, creating economic devastation and nationalist resentment that directly contributed to the political crisis of the Weimar Republic and the eventual rise of fascism — demonstrating how poorly designed peace settlements can plant the seeds of future wars.

The interwar period saw the rise of totalitarian ideologies that rejected liberal democracy: fascism (in Italy and Germany) combined extreme nationalism, racial hierarchy, corporatist economics, and charismatic dictatorship; Soviet communism under Stalin used Five Year Plans and collectivization to rapidly industrialize the USSR while eliminating perceived class enemies; and similar authoritarian systems spread across Eastern Europe, Asia, and Latin America.

The Great Depression (1929–1939) destroyed the liberal international economic order, collapsing trade, producing mass unemployment, and discrediting the laissez-faire capitalism that had dominated the previous century — governments responded with protectionism, state intervention (New Deal in the US), fascist corporatist economies (Germany, Italy), and Soviet-style planned industrialization.

World War II was caused by the combination of the unresolved legacy of WWI (Versailles grievances), the Great Depression's political radicalization, fascist expansionism, and collective security's failure (League of Nations' ineffectiveness) — producing a global conflict more destructive than WWI in which civilian targeting and mass atrocities became systematic.

The Holocaust, Armenian Genocide, Rwandan Genocide, and other 20th-century mass atrocities demonstrate how modern states can mobilize industrial technology, bureaucratic organization, and nationalist ideology to commit systematic extermination of targeted populations — creating the concept of 'genocide' (coined 1944) and the legal framework to prosecute perpetrators.
exam_tip: Unit 7 emphasizes causation and comparison — the AP exam frequently asks about the causes of WWI vs. WWII (and their similarities and differences), how totalitarian systems compared to each other, and how propaganda and total war mobilization operated in different national contexts. Pay close attention to how the Treaty of Versailles and Great Depression connected the two world wars, and practice identifying how mass atrocities from different contexts (Armenian Genocide, Holocaust, Rwanda) share structural similarities even while differing in specifics. The mandate system and its relationship to later decolonization is also frequently tested.
